The term animal electric signals refers to how living creatures use tiny bursts of electricity—like invisible sparks—to sense, communicate, or stun, without sound or touch.<[silence:30ms]>

clean_story:
Silent lightning. Instant messages.

The term animal electric signals refers to how living creatures use tiny bursts of electricity—like invisible sparks—to sense, communicate, or stun, without sound or touch.

A fish’s muscle twitch isn’t just movement; it’s a whisper in the dark. Electric eels stack thousands of modified muscle cells into batteries, firing 600 volts in a millisecond—enough to stun prey mid-swim. Weaker pulses, under 1 volt, let elephantfish map surroundings like sonar, detecting a hidden snail by its electrical shadow. Even bees carry static charges from flying, using them to stick pollen grains like magnets.

These signals aren’t noise. A platypus hunts with eyes closed, tracking shrimp by their nerve impulses’ faint voltage. Sharks read the bioelectric fields of wounded fish from meters away, like sniffing out a short circuit in the ocean.

Three related subjects are predator hunting tricks, bioelectric human medicine, and how nerves encode thoughts.
